title: Keynesian vs classical macroeconomics
summary:
## The big idea When a recession strikes, should the government spend its way out — or stand back and let wages and prices adjust? That question splits macroeconomics into its two great camps: **Keynesian** and **classical**. The disagreement is not really about diagrams; it's about **whether markets fix themselves**. Whichever LRAS shape you assume quietly decides the conclusion of every policy essay you write — so learn both, and say so. ## The classical view: the economy heals itself Classical (and later monetarist/new classical) economists trust **flexible prices and wages**. If demand falls and unemployment appears, wages fall; cheaper labour is rehired; the economy returns to full employment *on its own*. - **LRAS is vertical** at the full-employment ('natural') level of output — determined only by supply-side fundamentals: labour, capital, technology, productivity. - A rise in AD moves the economy up the vertical line: in the long run it delivers **only inflation** — same output, higher prices. So **demand management is pointless** (or worse). - The money supply matters through the **quantity theory of money, MV = PT**: with V (velocity) and T (transactions/output) roughly stable, growth in the money supply (M) feeds straight into the price level (P) — 'inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon'. - Policy programme: control money growth, keep budgets sound, and pursue **supply-side reform**. ## The Keynesian view: it can stay broken Keynes's Depression-era insight: wages are **sticky downward**. Workers and unions resist pay cuts; firms fear cutting morale — so the classical self-repair mechanism jams. The economy can settle into a **prolonged slump** with mass demand-deficient unemployment, waiting for a rescue that markets won't provide. - The **Keynesian AS curve is a reverse-L**: **horizontal** when there is deep spare capacity (extra demand → output, not prices), **upward-sloping** as bottlenecks appear, **vertical** only at full capacity. - With spare capacity, **AD management works**: government spending puts idle men and machines back to work with little inflation — and its effect is *multiplied*.
**What each part means:** the **yellow reverse-L** is the Keynesian AS: along its flat section (deep recession) extra demand raises **output** with almost no inflation; on the rising section bottlenecks push up both; at **Yfe** it turns vertical. The **dashed red vertical line** is the classical LRAS: the economy is always at (or quickly returns to) **Yfe**, so demand increases can only raise **prices**. Note the two curves *agree* at full employment — the fight is about what happens away from it, and how long 'away' lasts.
## The multiplier — Keynes's force multiplier An initial injection of spending doesn't stop at its first spending round: the government pays builders, builders buy groceries, grocers pay staff… each round of income is partly re-spent. The final rise in national income exceeds the injection: > **Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 − MPC) = 1 ÷ MPW** where **MPC** is the marginal propensity to consume and **MPW = MPS + MPT + MPM** (the leakages: saving, tax, imports). If MPC = 0.8, the multiplier is 5; if leakages are heavy (high taxes, import-hungry consumers), it shrinks. It works **in reverse** too — a spending cut multiplies downward. Small open economies have small multipliers (imports leak fast); that's a ready evaluation point. ## The accelerator — why investment is so jumpy Investment depends not on the *level* of output but its **rate of change**: firms add capacity only when demand is *growing*. A modest slowdown in sales growth can halve orders for new machinery — so investment swings far more violently than GDP, amplifying booms and busts. Multiplier and accelerator feeding each other is a classic story of the **trade cycle**. ## Money and interest rates — where the camps collide again Monetarists: money-supply growth beyond output growth → inflation (MV = PT); central banks should follow rules, not discretion. Keynesians: money matters, but monetary policy can fail exactly when needed most — in a **liquidity trap**, with interest rates near zero and confidence broken, cheap money just sits (Japan in the 1990s; much of the world after 2008) — leaving **fiscal policy** as the working tool. ## The synthesis — where exams want you to land Modern mainstream macro is a truce: **Keynesian in the short run, classical in the long run**. With spare capacity, demand policy restores output (2008–09's stimulus packages; 2020's pandemic responses); at full employment, extra demand is just inflation, and only **supply-side improvement** raises output durably. So the killer evaluation questions are always: *how much spare capacity exists?* and *what caused the problem?* — answer those, and the 'right' policy follows.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Classical**: flexible wages/prices → markets self-correct → **vertical LRAS** at Yfe → AD policy = inflation only; supply-side + money control; **MV = PT**. - **Keynesian**: wages **sticky downward** → economy can stick below Yfe → **reverse-L AS** → AD management works with spare capacity. - **Multiplier = 1/(1−MPC) = 1/MPW**; leakages (S+T+M) shrink it; works in reverse. - **Accelerator**: investment follows the *rate of change* of demand → investment volatility → trade cycle (with multiplier). - **Liquidity trap**: near-zero rates → monetary policy limp → fiscal takes over. - Synthesis: **Keynesian short run, classical long run** — judge by spare capacity and the cause of the problem.

keyTerms.definition: The view that markets clear and LRAS is vertical, so demand management is ineffective in the long run.
The view that sticky wages/prices can leave the economy below full employment, so AD management can work.
A reverse-L AS curve: horizontal with spare capacity, sloping up, then vertical at full capacity.
The ratio of the final change in national income to the initial change in spending; 1/(1−MPC).
The fraction of extra income that is spent; drives the size of the multiplier.
The theory that investment depends on the rate of change of output/demand.
The monetarist idea that excessive money-supply growth causes inflation.
When interest rates are so low that monetary policy loses traction on AD.

diagrams: **Classical vs Keynesian LRAS:** draw the vertical Classical LRAS and the reverse-L Keynesian LRAS; show that an AD rise causes only inflation on the vertical section but output gains on the horizontal section.
**The multiplier:** show an AD shift larger than the initial injection, illustrating the multiplied final effect on real output.
tips: Contrast the **two LRAS shapes** and explain how each changes the effect of a rise in AD — the crux of the debate.
Calculate the **multiplier = 1/(1−MPC) = 1/MPW** and explain why leakages (S, T, M) shrink it.
Link the **accelerator** to investment volatility and the trade cycle.
Reach a **synthesis judgement**: demand-side for spare capacity/short run, supply-side for long-run non-inflationary growth.
Note the **liquidity trap** and weak confidence as limits on monetary policy for evaluation.

revisionNotes:
# Keynesian vs classical — quick revision

**Classical / monetarist**
- Flexible wages & prices → markets clear → self-correction to full employment.
- **LRAS vertical** at Yfe (supply-side factors only). AD↑ → long-run inflation only.
- **MV = PT**: money growth beyond output → inflation. Policy: money discipline + supply-side.

**Keynesian**
- Wages **sticky downward** → demand-deficient unemployment can persist.
- **Reverse-L AS**: flat (spare capacity) → upward (bottlenecks) → vertical (Yfe).
- AD management effective in a slump; fiscal preferred in a **liquidity trap** (rates ≈ 0, monetary impotent).

**Multiplier**
- k = **1/(1−MPC) = 1/MPW**, MPW = MPS+MPT+MPM.
- MPC 0.8 → k = 5; heavy leakages (open, high-tax economies) → small k; reverse gear in downturns.

**Accelerator** — investment responds to the *rate of change* of output → amplifies cycles (with the multiplier).

**Synthesis (the exam judgement)**
- Short run/spare capacity → Keynesian demand policy works (2008, 2020).
- Long run/full employment → classical logic: only LRAS growth is non-inflationary.
- Always ask: how big is the output gap, and what caused the shock?
questions:
# Practice questions — Keynesian vs classical macroeconomics **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** In the classical model, an increase in aggregate demand in the long run causes: - A — higher output and unchanged prices - B — higher prices and unchanged output - C — higher output and lower prices - D — lower output and higher prices
▸ Show answer

B. LRAS is vertical at full employment: demand increases slide up the line — pure inflation, no lasting output gain.

**2.** An economy has MPS = 0.1, MPT = 0.2 and MPM = 0.2. The value of the multiplier is: - A — 2 - B — 5 - C — 0.5 - D — 10
▸ Show answer

A. MPW = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.5; multiplier = 1/MPW = 2. A \$1bn injection finally raises income by \$2bn.

**3.** The Keynesian argument that recessions can persist rests principally on: - A — perfectly flexible wages - B — wages being sticky in the downward direction - C — the quantity theory of money - D — a vertical long-run aggregate supply curve
▸ Show answer

B. If wages won't fall, the labour market can't clear, so demand-deficient unemployment persists — breaking the classical self-correction story.

**4.** The accelerator theory predicts that a slowdown in the growth of consumer demand will cause: - A — a proportionately larger fall in investment - B — a proportionately smaller fall in investment - C — no change in investment - D — an immediate rise in the money supply
▸ Show answer

A. Investment depends on the change in output: firms stop adding capacity as soon as growth merely slows — so investment swings harder than GDP.

## Paper 4 style — data response > A large economy falls into recession: output is 6% below capacity, unemployment has doubled, inflation is 0.5%, and the central bank's policy rate is 0.25%. Business surveys show firms hoarding cash despite cheap credit. One adviser urges a major public investment programme, estimating MPC at 0.75; another insists 'the economy will right itself if wages are allowed to adjust — borrowing will only create inflation and debt'. **(a)** Define the 'multiplier'. **[2]** **(b)** Calculate the multiplier implied by the first adviser's estimate, and the final impact of a \$40 billion investment programme. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse why monetary policy appears ineffective in this economy. **[6]** **(d)** Assess which adviser the government should follow. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) The ratio of the final change in national income to the initial change in injection spending. [1+1]

(b) k = 1/(1−0.75) = 4; final impact = 4 × \$40bn = \$160bn. (Note leakages via tax/imports would shrink this — creditable qualification.) [4]

(c) Liquidity-trap conditions: rates already near zero (0.25%) leave no room to cut; firms hoard cash because expected returns are poor — demand for funds, not their price, is the constraint; banks may also ration credit. Monetary transmission (rate → C, I) breaks at every link. [6]

(d) Keynesian case: large output gap (6%), negligible inflation, broken monetary policy → conditions where fiscal stimulus is most effective and least inflationary; multiplier of 4 with idle resources. Classical worry: debt, future crowding out, wage adjustment — but downward wage flexibility is empirically weak and deflation risk (0.5% inflation) makes waiting dangerous. Judgement: adviser 1 in the short run, with credible long-run consolidation. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain how the shape of the aggregate supply curve differs between the Keynesian and classical models, and why this matters for the effect of an increase in aggregate demand. **[8]** **(b)** Assess which model provides better guidance for policy in a deep recession. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Reverse-L vs vertical; AD rise on flat section → output; on vertical → prices; diagram with both curves.

(b) Keynesian: sticky wages, liquidity traps and the 1930s/2008/2020 record support stimulus in slumps. Classical: warns of debt, lags, crowding out and the inflation that follows over-stimulus (1970s; post-2021). Judgement: Keynesian for deep slumps with spare capacity; classical disciplines the long run — capacity and cause decide.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain how the multiplier and accelerator interact to amplify fluctuations in national income. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether a government can rely on the multiplier effect when planning fiscal stimulus. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Injection → multiplied consumption rounds (1/(1−MPC)); rising output → accelerator investment surge → further multiplier rounds — and the reverse on the way down (trade-cycle mechanism).

(b) For: with spare capacity and high MPC, well-targeted spending multiplies reliably. Against: size uncertain (leakages — imports, saving, tax; small open economies), time lags, crowding out near capacity, Ricardian consumers saving tax cuts, measurement only after the fact. Judgement: usable but with wide error bars — target high-MPC recipients and domestic content.
